content:
# Factors Affecting Pseudo Relative Permeability Curves

**Summary**: The thesis investigates the impact of various reservoir parameters—including PVT properties, layer thickness, dip angle, and absolute permeability—on the generation of pseudo relative permeability curves in sandstone reservoirs. The study concludes that while factors like vertical-to-horizontal permeability ratio and production rate have minimal effects, reservoir dip angle and layer thickness significantly influence the resulting curves.

---

## Research Problem

Large-scale reservoirs in Saudi Arabia require high grid counts for accurate simulation, but many are modeled with coarse grids to save costs. This leads to a loss of information regarding vertical heterogeneity; pseudo relative permeability curves are used to compensate for this, but the specific factors (rock, fluid, or structure) that influence these curves are not fully defined.

## Objectives

- Identify the factors affecting pseudo relative permeability curves.
- Study the effect of different fluids, rock and structure properties on pseudo relative permeability curves in a study to reduce the number of pseudo relative permeability curves necessary for such reservoirs.
- To determine the impact of vertical to horizontal permeability ratio (Kv/Kx) on pseudo relative permeability.
- To study the influence of model layer thickness on pseudo relative permeability curves.
- To analyze the effect of reservoir dip angle on pseudo relative permeability.
- To investigate the role of absolute permeability in the generation of pseudo relative permeability curves.

## Methods

The study utilized cross-sectional models to generate pseudo relative permeability curves. Two sets of PVT data (PVT1 and PVT2) were compared across different reservoir configurations. Parameters such as layer thickness (50 ft vs 110 ft), dip angles (1° and 4°), and Kv/Kx ratios (0.3 and 0.7) were varied systematically. A validation step involved comparing these pseudo curves against a coarse 2-D model to ensure they accurately represented the water saturation front compared to standard rock relative permeability.

## Data and Materials

The study used two sets of PVT data (PVT1 and PVT2), cross-sectional models with varying thicknesses (50 ft and 110 ft), dip angles (1° and 4°), and Kv/Kx ratios (0.3 and 0.7). Production rates were tested at 1.1% of original oil in-place per year, with some cases varied by a factor of 1.5.

## Key Findings

- The most pronounced effect on pseudo relative permeability curves is caused by the reservoir dip angle. ([[1992-al-otaibi|Al-Otaibi, 1992, p. 18]])
- Layer thickness and PVT properties also have an effect on the curves. ([[1992-al-otaibi|Al-Otaibi, 1992, p. 18]])
- Production rate and vertical to horizontal permeability ratio (Kv/Kx) have no significant effect on the parameter ranges studied. ([[1992-al-otaibi|Al-Otaibi, 1992, p. 18]])
- For low dip angle cases, absolute permeability has no effect; however, at high dip angle cases, absolute permeability must be taken into consideration. ([[1992-al-otaibi|Al-Otaibi, 1992, p. 18]])
- The Kyte and Berry method is the most widely used in the industry for calculating dynamic pseudo relative permeability functions. ([[1992-al-otaibi|Al-Otaibi, 1992, p. 21]])
- PVT properties have an effect on pseudo relative permeability curves; the effect is minimal for large thickness (110 ft) and low dip angles (1°), but maximum for small thickness (50 ft) and high dip angles (4°). ([[1992-al-otaibi|Al-Otaibi, 1992, p. 59]])
- The ratio of vertical to horizontal permeability (Kv/Kx) has no effect on pseudo relative permeability curves regardless of reservoir dip angle or layer thickness. ([[1992-al-otaibi|Al-Otaibi, 1992, p. 64]])
- Layer thickness affects the curves; smaller thicknesses result in higher oil relative permeability values at the same water saturation and a shift of the intersection toward higher water saturation. ([[1992-al-otaibi|Al-Otaibi, 1992, p. 73]])
- The reservoir dip angle has the most significant influence on pseudo relative permeability curves among all studied factors, with higher angles leading to better sweep and higher oil relative permeability values. ([[1992-al-otaibi|Al-Otaibi, 1992, p. 86]])
- Absolute permeability only significantly affects results in cases where the dip angle is high; for low dip angles, the effect is negligible. ([[1992-al-otaibi|Al-Otaibi, 1992, p. 91]])
- Production rates within the tested range (up to a factor of 1.5 increase) had no significant effect on the observed pseudo relative permeability curves. ([[1992-al-otaibi|Al-Otaibi, 1992, p. 108]])

## Limitations

Not stated in source

## Recommendations and Future Work

- For sandstone reservoirs where PVT data changes with depth, separate sets of pseudo relative permeability curves are required.
- For reservoir simulation where layer thickness varies, specific pseudo relative permeability curves for each thickness should be used.
- Pseudo relative permeability curves should be generated specifically for areas where the reservoir dip angle changes if absolute permeability also varies.
